stars.Sirius: The brightest star in the entire night sky, the 'Dog Star' of Canis Major. It has a white-dwarf companion, Sirius B, nicknamed 'the Pup'.
stars.Canopus: The second-brightest star, a distant white supergiant so steady that spacecraft use it as a navigation beacon.
stars.Arcturus: The brightest star of the northern sky, an aging orange giant speeding through the galaxy. Follow the arc of the Big Dipper's handle to find it.
stars.Vega: A brilliant blue-white star that was the pole star around 12,000 BC and will be again near 13,700 AD. It once defined magnitude zero.
stars.Capella: The brightest star in Auriga and sixth-brightest overall — actually two pairs of stars orbiting together.
stars.Rigel: A blue supergiant marking Orion's foot, tens of thousands of times more luminous than the Sun.
stars.Procyon: The eighth-brightest star, with a faint white-dwarf companion. It forms the Winter Triangle with Sirius and Betelgeuse.
stars.Betelgeuse: A colossal red supergiant on Orion's shoulder — so vast it would swallow the inner planets. It will one day explode as a supernova.
stars.Achernar: A fast-spinning blue star at the end of the river Eridanus, flung into an oval by its own rotation.
stars.Altair: One of the closest naked-eye stars; it spins so fast that a day there lasts only about nine hours. It marks the Summer Triangle with Vega and Deneb.
stars.Aldebaran: The fiery orange eye of Taurus the bull — an aging giant that appears among, but lies closer than, the Hyades cluster.
stars.Antares: A vast red supergiant whose name means 'rival of Mars' for its ruddy glow. It is the beating heart of the Scorpion.
stars.Spica: A close pair of hot blue stars that whirl around each other; the brightest star in Virgo.
stars.Pollux: The brighter of the Gemini twins, an orange giant known to host a planet.
stars.Fomalhaut: The 'lonely star of autumn', encircled by a vast ring of dusty debris.
stars.Deneb: One of the most luminous stars known — a distant white supergiant marking the tail of Cygnus the swan.
stars.Regulus: The 'little king' at the heart of Leo, a fast-spinning blue-white star lying almost exactly on the ecliptic.
stars.Castor: The fainter Gemini twin — in fact a remarkable system of six stars bound together.
stars.Bellatrix: The 'female warrior', a hot blue star on Orion's western shoulder.
stars.Polaris: The North Star, poised almost directly over Earth's north pole so it barely moves as the sky wheels around it. It is a pulsating Cepheid variable.
stars.Mizar: The middle star of the Big Dipper's handle; with nearby Alcor it forms a classic naked-eye double.
stars.Alcor: The faint companion of Mizar, long used as a test of keen eyesight.
stars.Algol: The 'Demon Star', which visibly dims every 2.9 days as a companion eclipses it — the first eclipsing binary ever recognised.
stars.Alnilam: The central, brightest star of Orion's Belt, a distant blue supergiant.
stars.Alnitak: The eastern star of Orion's Belt, set near the Flame and Horsehead nebulae.
stars.Mintaka: The western star of Orion's Belt, lying almost exactly on the celestial equator.
stars.Albireo: The head of Cygnus — through a telescope a gorgeous double of a golden and a blue star.
stars.Rigil Kentaurus: Alpha Centauri, the closest star system to the Sun at 4.4 light-years; its faint red companion Proxima is the nearest star of all.
stars.Hadar: Also called Agena; a blue giant that, with Alpha Centauri, points the way to the Southern Cross.
stars.Dubhe: One of the two 'Pointer' stars on the lip of the Big Dipper's bowl that line up on Polaris.
stars.Merak: The lower 'Pointer' star of the Big Dipper, guiding the eye up to the North Star.
stars.Alkaid: The star at the very tip of the Big Dipper's handle.
stars.Alioth: The brightest star of Ursa Major, where the Dipper's handle meets its bowl.
stars.Thuban: A modest star in Draco that served as the pole star of the ancient Egyptians around 3000 BC.
stars.Denebola: The star marking the tail of Leo the lion.
stars.Alphard: The 'solitary one', the single bright orange star in the sprawling, dim constellation Hydra.
stars.Hamal: The brightest star of Aries the ram.
stars.Mirfak: The brightest star of Perseus, a supergiant set within a loose scattering of stars.
stars.Alphecca: The jewel at the centre of the Northern Crown, Corona Borealis.
stars.Rasalhague: The 'head of the serpent-charmer', the brightest star in Ophiuchus.
stars.Nunki: A hot blue star in the handle of Sagittarius's Teapot.
stars.Shaula: The 'stinger' in the raised tail of Scorpius.
stars.Sadr: The star at the heart of the Northern Cross, embedded in rich Milky Way star fields.
stars.Enif: The nose of Pegasus the winged horse.
stars.Markab: A corner star of the Great Square of Pegasus.
stars.Scheat: A red giant at a corner of the Great Square of Pegasus.
stars.Alpheratz: A corner star shared between Pegasus's Great Square and the chained princess Andromeda.
stars.Diphda: Also called Deneb Kaitos, the tail of Cetus the sea monster.
stars.Adhara: The second-brightest star of Canis Major; millions of years ago it passed close to the Sun and was then the brightest star in our sky.
stars.Wezen: A yellow-white supergiant in Canis Major, thousands of times more luminous than the Sun.
stars.Naos: One of the hottest and most luminous naked-eye stars, a searing blue O-type star in Puppis.
stars.Alnair: The brightest star of Grus the crane.
stars.Peacock: The lead star of Pavo the peacock.
stars.Acrux: The brightest star of the Southern Cross and the southernmost first-magnitude star.
stars.Gacrux: The nearby red giant at the top of the Southern Cross.
stars.Mimosa: Also called Becrux, the second-brightest star of the Southern Cross.
stars.Elnath: The northern horn of Taurus, a star historically shared with neighbouring Auriga.
stars.Menkalinan: The second-brightest star of Auriga the charioteer.
stars.Saiph: Marks Orion's second foot — a hot blue supergiant much like Rigel.
stars.Eltanin: The head of Draco the dragon, once known as the 'Zenith Star' as it passed overhead in London.

constellations.And: Andromeda, the chained princess of Greek myth rescued by Perseus. It is home to the Andromeda Galaxy, the most distant thing visible to the naked eye.
constellations.Ant: Antlia, the air pump — a faint modern constellation named by Lacaille for a scientific instrument.
constellations.Aps: Apus, the bird of paradise, a dim southern constellation near the celestial pole.
constellations.Aqr: Aquarius, the water-bearer of the zodiac, pouring a stream of water toward the Southern Fish.
constellations.Aql: Aquila, the eagle that carried the thunderbolts of Zeus. Its brightest star is Altair.
constellations.Ara: Ara, the altar on which the gods swore their alliance before the war against the Titans.
constellations.Ari: Aries, the ram of the Golden Fleece and the traditional first constellation of the zodiac.
constellations.Aur: Auriga, the charioteer, crowned by the brilliant golden star Capella.
constellations.Boo: Boötes, the herdsman driving the bears around the pole, anchored by fiery orange Arcturus.
constellations.Cae: Caelum, the engraver's chisel — one of the smallest and faintest constellations.
constellations.Cam: Camelopardalis, the giraffe, a large but very dim northern constellation.
constellations.Cnc: Cancer, the crab crushed beneath the heel of Hercules; a faint zodiac sign holding the Beehive Cluster.
constellations.CVn: Canes Venatici, the hunting dogs held on a leash by Boötes.
constellations.CMa: Canis Major, the great dog, home to Sirius — the brightest star in the night sky.
constellations.CMi: Canis Minor, the little dog, marked by the bright star Procyon.
constellations.Cap: Capricornus, the sea-goat, an ancient and faint constellation of the zodiac.
constellations.Car: Carina, the keel of the great ship Argo. It contains Canopus and the vast Eta Carinae Nebula.
constellations.Cas: Cassiopeia, the vain queen chained to her throne, forming an unmistakable 'W' opposite the Big Dipper.
constellations.Cen: Centaurus, the wise centaur Chiron. It hosts Alpha Centauri, the nearest star system, and brilliant Omega Centauri.
constellations.Cep: Cepheus, the king of Aethiopia, a house-shaped figure near the north pole.
constellations.Cet: Cetus, the sea monster sent to devour Andromeda. It contains the famous variable star Mira.
constellations.Cha: Chamaeleon, the chameleon, a small faint constellation close to the south celestial pole.
constellations.Cir: Circinus, the drafting compasses, a tiny southern constellation beside Alpha Centauri.
constellations.Col: Columba, the dove — said to represent the dove Noah released from the ark.
constellations.Com: Coma Berenices, the flowing hair of Queen Berenice. It holds a nearby star cluster and a swarm of distant galaxies.
constellations.CrA: Corona Austrina, the southern crown, a delicate arc of stars below Sagittarius.
constellations.CrB: Corona Borealis, the northern crown — a graceful semicircle, the jewelled crown of Ariadne.
constellations.Crv: Corvus, the crow sent by Apollo, a compact and distinctive quadrilateral of stars.
constellations.Crt: Crater, the cup of Apollo, resting on the back of Hydra next to Corvus.
constellations.Cru: Crux, the Southern Cross — the smallest constellation, and a key signpost pointing toward the south pole.
constellations.Cyg: Cygnus, the swan flying down the Milky Way. Its bright stars also form the Northern Cross, tipped by Deneb.
constellations.Del: Delphinus, the dolphin, a small but charming diamond of stars.
constellations.Dor: Dorado, the swordfish, home to most of the Large Magellanic Cloud.
constellations.Dra: Draco, the dragon, coiling its long body around the north celestial pole.
constellations.Equ: Equuleus, the little horse — the second-smallest of all the constellations.
constellations.Eri: Eridanus, the celestial river, winding from Orion's foot far south to brilliant Achernar.
constellations.For: Fornax, the chemical furnace, a faint constellation that is a window onto very distant galaxies.
constellations.Gem: Gemini, the twins Castor and Pollux, a bright constellation of the zodiac.
constellations.Gru: Grus, the crane, a graceful southern bird constellation.
constellations.Her: Hercules, the kneeling hero of a dozen labours. It holds M13, the finest globular cluster of the northern sky.
constellations.Hor: Horologium, the pendulum clock, a faint winding southern constellation.
constellations.Hya: Hydra, the water snake — the largest constellation of all, sprawling across a quarter of the sky.
constellations.Hyi: Hydrus, the little water snake, tucked near the south celestial pole.
constellations.Ind: Indus, the Indian, a southern constellation from the age of European exploration.
constellations.Lac: Lacerta, the lizard, a small zigzag of faint stars along the Milky Way.
constellations.Leo: Leo, the Nemean lion slain by Hercules. Its curved 'Sickle' forms the lion's head, anchored by Regulus.
constellations.LMi: Leo Minor, the lesser lion, a faint constellation crouched north of Leo.
constellations.Lep: Lepus, the hare, crouching quietly beneath the feet of Orion.
constellations.Lib: Libra, the scales of justice — the only inanimate constellation of the zodiac.
constellations.Lup: Lupus, the wolf, an ancient constellation of the southern sky.
constellations.Lyn: Lynx, the lynx, so named because it is said you need the eyes of a lynx to see it.
constellations.Lyr: Lyra, the lyre of Orpheus, dominated by the brilliant blue-white star Vega.
constellations.Men: Mensa, Table Mountain — the faintest constellation, holding a corner of the Large Magellanic Cloud.
constellations.Mic: Microscopium, the microscope, a faint southern constellation.
constellations.Mon: Monoceros, the unicorn, straddling the Milky Way just east of Orion.
constellations.Mus: Musca, the fly, a small southern constellation buzzing beneath the Southern Cross.
constellations.Nor: Norma, the carpenter's level and square, set in a rich part of the Milky Way.
constellations.Oct: Octans, the octant — the constellation that contains the south celestial pole itself.
constellations.Oph: Ophiuchus, the serpent-bearer, representing the great healer Asclepius holding a snake.
constellations.Ori: Orion, the great hunter — the most recognisable constellation, with his three-star Belt and the glowing Orion Nebula hanging from it.
constellations.Pav: Pavo, the peacock, a bright southern constellation.
constellations.Peg: Pegasus, the winged horse. Its Great Square is a key signpost of the autumn sky.
constellations.Per: Perseus, the hero who slew Medusa. It holds the Double Cluster and the winking demon-star Algol.
constellations.Phe: Phoenix, the firebird forever reborn from its own ashes.
constellations.Pic: Pictor, the painter's easel, a faint southern constellation beside Canopus.
constellations.Psc: Pisces, the two fish tied together by their tails; a faint zodiac constellation marking the spring equinox.
constellations.PsA: Piscis Austrinus, the southern fish drinking the stream of Aquarius, marked by lonely Fomalhaut.
constellations.Pup: Puppis, the stern of the great ship Argo.
constellations.Pyx: Pyxis, the mariner's compass, a small faint constellation.
constellations.Ret: Reticulum, the reticle of an eyepiece, a small southern constellation.
constellations.Sge: Sagitta, the arrow — the third-smallest constellation, a tidy little dart in the Milky Way.
constellations.Sgr: Sagittarius, the archer aiming at the Scorpion. Its Teapot pattern points toward the centre of our galaxy.
constellations.Sco: Scorpius, the scorpion that killed Orion. This bright zodiac constellation is led by the red supergiant Antares.
constellations.Scl: Sculptor, the sculptor's studio, containing the south galactic pole and a nearby group of galaxies.
constellations.Sct: Scutum, the shield, home to one of the richest star clouds of the Milky Way.
constellations.Ser: Serpens, the serpent held by Ophiuchus — the only constellation split in two, a Head (Caput) and a Tail (Cauda).
constellations.Sex: Sextans, the sextant, a faint equatorial constellation south of Leo.
constellations.Tau: Taurus, the bull charging at Orion. It carries the Pleiades and Hyades clusters and the red giant Aldebaran as its eye.
constellations.Tel: Telescopium, the telescope, a faint southern constellation.
constellations.Tri: Triangulum, the triangle, a small but ancient constellation, home to the Triangulum Galaxy.
constellations.TrA: Triangulum Australe, the southern triangle — brighter and bolder than its northern namesake.
constellations.Tuc: Tucana, the toucan, holding the Small Magellanic Cloud and the great globular cluster 47 Tucanae.
constellations.UMa: Ursa Major, the great bear, which contains the Big Dipper — the most famous star pattern in the sky.
constellations.UMi: Ursa Minor, the little bear, whose Little Dipper is tipped by Polaris, the North Star.
constellations.Vel: Vela, the sails of the great ship Argo.
constellations.Vir: Virgo, the maiden holding an ear of wheat. This large zodiac constellation shines with Spica and hosts a huge cluster of galaxies.
constellations.Vol: Volans, the flying fish, gliding beside the Large Magellanic Cloud.
constellations.Vul: Vulpecula, the little fox, home to the Dumbbell Nebula and the Coathanger asterism.

Cr 140.text: A scattered open cluster of young stars in Canis Major, easily swept up with binoculars.

Cr 399.text: The Coathanger — a chance line-up of stars in Vulpecula that really does look like an upside-down coat hanger.

IC 2602.text: The 'Southern Pleiades', a bright, sparse cluster of hot young stars in Carina.

LMC.text: The Large Magellanic Cloud, a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way and a naked-eye smudge from the southern hemisphere.

M 4.text: One of the nearest globular clusters — a dense ball of hundreds of thousands of ancient stars, right beside Antares.

M 6.text: The Butterfly Cluster, a bright open cluster whose stars trace the shape of a butterfly.

M 7.text: The Ptolemy Cluster, a big bright open cluster noted by Ptolemy nearly 2,000 years ago.

M 8.text: The Lagoon Nebula, a glowing cloud of gas in Sagittarius where new stars are being born.

M 16.text: The Eagle Nebula, a stellar nursery famous for the towering 'Pillars of Creation'.

M 31.text: The Andromeda Galaxy — the nearest large galaxy and the most distant object visible to the unaided eye, on a collision course with the Milky Way.

M 33.text: The Triangulum Galaxy, the third-largest member of our Local Group of galaxies.

M 42.text: The Orion Nebula, a vast glowing nursery of newborn stars — the fuzzy 'star' in Orion's Sword, visible to the naked eye.

M 44.text: The Beehive Cluster (Praesepe), a swarm of stars in Cancer that looks like a hazy patch to the naked eye.

M 45.text: The Pleiades or Seven Sisters, the most famous open cluster — a tight knot of blue stars wrapped in faint dust.

Mel 20.text: The Alpha Persei Cluster, a bright loose scattering of stars around Mirfak.

Mel 25.text: The Hyades, the nearest open cluster, forming the V-shaped face of Taurus the bull.

Mel 111.text: The Coma Star Cluster, a large, loose sprinkling of stars best seen with the naked eye or binoculars.

47 Tuc.text: 47 Tucanae, the second-brightest globular cluster in the sky, a dazzling ball of a million ancient stars.

h Per.text: One half of the Double Cluster in Perseus — twin rich open clusters visible together in binoculars.

χ Per.text: The companion of the Double Cluster in Perseus, packed with hot young stars.

NGC 2244.text: The open cluster at the heart of the Rosette Nebula, its young stars lighting up the surrounding gas.

NGC 2264.text: The Christmas Tree Cluster and Cone Nebula, a young star-forming region in Monoceros.

NGC 2362.text: A compact, very young open cluster surrounding the bright star Tau Canis Majoris.

NGC 2451.text: A bright open cluster in Puppis with a conspicuous orange star at its centre.

NGC 2516.text: A rich, bright open cluster in Carina, sometimes called the Southern Beehive.

η Car.text: The Eta Carinae Nebula, one of the largest glowing gas clouds in the sky, wrapped around the unstable, doomed supergiant Eta Carinae.

NGC 3532.text: The Wishing Well Cluster, a glittering open cluster in Carina and a favourite binocular target.

ω Cen.text: Omega Centauri, the brightest and largest globular cluster in our galaxy — up to ten million stars, possibly the core of a swallowed dwarf galaxy.

NGC 6231.text: A brilliant cluster of hot, massive young stars in the tail of Scorpius.

SMC.text: The Small Magellanic Cloud, a companion galaxy of the Milky Way, visible as a soft glow in southern skies.

IC 2391.text: The Omicron Velorum Cluster, a bright, nearby open cluster easily seen with the naked eye.

GalCtr.text: The centre of our Milky Way galaxy, in the direction of Sagittarius — home to Sagittarius A*, a supermassive black hole four million times the Sun's mass.

Sun.subtitle: Our star — a G-type yellow dwarf
facts: ~4.6 billion years (about halfway through its life)
Sun.text: The Sun is the star at the centre of our solar system, fusing 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium every second. Never look at it directly — but it makes an easy target for checking the app's alignment.
Sun.warn: Never point optics at the Sun or look at it directly.

Moon.subtitle: Earth's only natural satellite
facts: Rock and dust over an iron-rich core
Moon.text: The Moon shines only by reflected sunlight; the changing angle of the Sun gives us its phases. The same face always points toward Earth.

Mercury.subtitle: The smallest planet, closest to the Sun
facts: Iron core under a thin rocky crust
Mercury.text: A scorched, cratered world that never strays far from the Sun, so it is only ever glimpsed low in twilight.

Venus.subtitle: The brilliant 'morning/evening star'
Venus.text: The brightest planet, wrapped in reflective clouds of sulphuric acid. A runaway greenhouse effect makes it the hottest planet of all.

Mars.subtitle: The Red Planet
facts: Iron-oxide dust over rock; thin CO₂ air
Mars.text: Rusty iron dust gives Mars its red colour. It hosts the tallest volcano and the largest canyon in the solar system, and two tiny moons.

Jupiter.subtitle: The largest planet
facts: Mostly hydrogen and helium — a failed star
95+ known, including the four Galilean moons
Jupiter.text: A giant ball of gas that could swallow all the other planets combined. Its Great Red Spot is a storm wider than Earth, raging for centuries.

Saturn.subtitle: The ringed planet
Saturn.text: Famous for its magnificent rings of ice and rock. Saturn is so light it would float in water, and has more than 140 known moons.

Uranus.subtitle: The tilted ice giant
facts: Water, methane and ammonia ices over rock
Uranus.text: A cold, blue-green world tipped completely on its side, so each pole spends decades in sunlight then decades in darkness. Just barely visible to the naked eye under dark skies.

Neptune.subtitle: The windiest planet
facts: Water, methane and ammonia ices over rock
Neptune.text: The most distant planet, a deep-blue ice giant with the fiercest winds in the solar system. It was found by mathematics before it was ever seen, and needs a telescope to spot.
